weeks.weekly_summary: Foundation week establishing aerobic base with easy runs and introductory strides. Low volume (30.5 km) over 5 days allows adaptation from recent light training load.
weeks.coaching_notes: Athlete has run only ~10 km in past 14 days, indicating a fresh start or recent layoff. This week is intentionally conservative to build momentum without overtraining. The 3.6 km and 6.3 km runs from late June show easy paces (5:50–6:10/km), so we establish a conversational baseline around 6:15/km for Zone 2. Two quality sessions (strides) introduce neuromuscular activation without fatigue.
recovery_flags: Recent training volume very low; watch for DOMS in week 2.
No HR data available; use perceived effort and pace as guides.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 2 builds to 38 km across 5 runs, introducing a tempo session and maintaining 2 stride workouts. Long run grows to 12 km.

days.title: Easy 6 km
days.description: Relaxed aerobic run at conversational pace. Focus on smooth cadence and form.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery: light stretching, foam rolling, or walk if desired.

days.title: Easy 5 km + 6×100m Strides
days.description: 5 km easy run at 6:15/km pace, followed by 6 × 100 m strides (20–25 sec effort, walk 90 sec recovery). Strides activate neuromuscular system without metabolic stress.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery or complete rest.

days.title: Easy 5 km + 6×100m Strides
days.description: Repeat Wednesday structure: 5 km easy, then 6 × 100 m strides. Maintains neuromuscular stimulus while respecting recovery.
days.notes: Second quality session; 48 hours from Wednesday session.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest or very light activity (yoga, walk).

days.title: Long Run 8.5 km
days.description: Aerobic long run at comfortable, conversational pace (~6:20/km). Focus on steady effort and finishing feeling strong.
days.notes: Modest long run; will grow each week. Stay hydrated.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume increases to 38 km across 5 runs. Introduces first tempo session (6×2 min @ Z3) to develop aerobic power while maintaining easy run base. Long run grows to 12 km.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 1 establishes rhythm; week 2 adds stimulus with a tempo workout. The 6×2 min tempo (120 sec hard, 90 sec easy recovery) develops lactate threshold without excessive fatigue. Polar distribution: ~30.4 km easy (80%), ~7.6 km hard (20%). Monitor fatigue after Wednesday tempo; ensure Thursday is easy or rest.
recovery_flags: First tempo session; observe RPE and how body responds.
Total weekly hours will be ~3.8–4.0 hrs (within 7-hour budget).
Maintain 48+ hours between Wednesday tempo and Sunday long run.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 3 expands to 44 km, building longer tempo (3×4 min) and introducing hill repeats. Long run reaches 14 km.

days.title: Easy 6.5 km
days.description: Relaxed aerobic run at 6:15/km. Start the week fresh and easy.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest or very light cross-training (yoga, stretching).

days.title: 6 km Easy + 6×2 min Tempo + 1 km Easy
days.description: 6 km easy warm-up (6:15/km), then 6 × 2 min at tempo pace (~5:30/km, hard but sustainable) with 90 sec easy jog recovery between repeats. Finish with 1 km easy cool-down. Total tempo time: 12 min.
days.notes: First tempo session of block. Expected HR ~80–88% max. Comfortably hard—can speak 1–2 words.

days.title: Easy 6.5 km
days.description: Recovery run following Wednesday tempo. Keep pace easy and conversational. Promotes blood flow and active recovery.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest before weekend running.

days.title: Easy 7 km
days.description: Steady easy run at 6:15/km. Build confidence into Sunday's longer effort.

days.title: Long Run 12 km
days.description: Aerobic long run at conversational pace (6:20/km). Steady, sustainable effort focusing on time on feet. Hydrate at 4 km and 8 km marks.
days.notes: First 12 km run; expect some fatigue—this is normal. Finish strong.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume peaks at 44 km across 5 runs. Tempo session extends to 3×4 min; hill repeats added for VO2 max development. Long run reaches 14 km with steady progression.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 3 marks transition from Base Building to Aerobic Development phase. Tempo extends to 3×4 min (240 sec hard, ~2 min recovery) to stress lactate threshold further. Hill repeats (6×90 sec) introduce VO2 max stimulus and build power. Polar distribution: ~35.2 km easy, ~8.8 km hard. Total weekly time ~4.5 hrs (still within budget). Monitor for cumulative fatigue; ensure adequate sleep and nutrition.
recovery_flags: Longest week of block; watch for overtraining signs (HRV drop, elevated RHR, mood changes).
Hill repeats are demanding; ensure Wednesday (hill) and Thursday (easy) spacing is respected.
Long run now 14 km—significant aerobic load. Fuel properly.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 4 reduces volume slightly to 40 km (deload/consolidation week before any subsequent block). Tempo becomes 4×3 min for intensity maintenance. Prepares for next macrocycle.

days.title: Easy 7 km
days.description: Easy start to peak week. 6:15/km conversational pace.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest before tempo session.

days.title: 6 km Easy + 3×4 min Tempo + 1 km Easy
days.description: 6 km warm-up (6:15/km), then 3 × 4 min at tempo pace (~5:30/km) with 2 min easy jog recovery between repeats. Finish with 1 km cool-down. Total tempo time: 12 min (sustained harder work than week 2).
days.notes: Longer repeats demand concentration and pacing discipline. Stay smooth.

days.title: 5 km Easy + 6×90 sec Hill Repeats + 1 km Easy
days.description: 5 km easy warm-up on flat ground, then 6 × 90 sec hard uphill (moderate-to-steep gradient, ~4–5% grade if possible) with 90 sec easy jog/walk downhill recovery. Finish with 1 km easy cool-down. Focus on controlled breathing and strong leg drive uphill.
days.key_focus: VO2 max development, leg power, mental toughness
days.notes: Hills are demanding; RPE should be 8–9/10 on repeats. This is a second hard session—only possible because Wednesday and Thursday are separated and both are high-quality without a long run.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full recovery before weekend long run.

days.title: Easy 7.5 km
days.description: Easy run to warm up for long run. Builds aerobic momentum into Sunday.

days.title: Long Run 14 km
days.description: Aerobic long run at steady, conversational pace (~6:20/km). Significant mental and physiological stimulus. Hydrate every 4 km; consider light fuel (sports drink or energy gels) after 8 km if running >90 min.
days.key_focus: Aerobic endurance, time-on-feet adaptation, mental strength
days.notes: Longest run yet. Finish strong; this builds aerobic capacity for marathon prep.

weeks.weekly_summary: Consolidation week at 40 km across 5 runs. Volume drops 10% from week 3 to allow adaptation and prevent overtraining. Tempo reduced to 4×3 min; no hills. Long run holds at 14 km (not progressive). Prepares for next macrocycle.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 4 is a planned deload within the base-building block. After week 3's peak (44 km with high intensity), this week reduces volume and intensity to allow physiological adaptation. Tempo becomes 4×3 min (120 sec/repeat) instead of longer repeats; no hills. Long run repeats at 14 km rather than extending, consolidating aerobic gains. Total time ~4.1 hrs. This sets the athlete up fresh for the next 4-week block (Aerobic Development or Peak, depending on race timeline).
recovery_flags: Deload week—expected to feel fresher by end of week.
Lower intensity allows glycogen repletion and adaptation.
Monitor fatigue scores; if low, consider extending training plan into peak phase next block.
weeks.next_week_preview: Following this block, athlete should begin 2nd macrocycle (Aerobic Development with longer tempos, marathon-pace runs) if race is 8–12 weeks out, or Progress to Peak Phase (marathon-pace segments, VO2 max maintenance) if race is 5–7 weeks away.

days.title: Easy 6.5 km
days.description: Easy start to deload week. Keep pace relaxed at 6:15/km.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest or light mobility work.

days.title: 6 km Easy + 4×3 min Tempo + 1 km Easy
days.description: 6 km warm-up (6:15/km), then 4 × 3 min at tempo pace (~5:30/km) with 2 min easy recovery jogs. Finish with 1 km cool-down. Total tempo time: 12 min (same total hard time as week 3, but fewer repeats = less accumulated stress).
days.notes: Reduced repeat count from week 3; intensity maintained but volume down.

days.title: Easy 6 km
days.description: Recovery run following Wednesday tempo. Very relaxed effort.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest before weekend.

days.title: Easy 6.5 km
days.description: Easy build-up before long run. Conversational pace.

days.title: Long Run 14 km
days.description: Consolidation long run at steady aerobic pace (~6:20/km). Same distance as week 3, reinforcing the aerobic adaptation without progression. This is a controlled, confident run—finish strong and ready for next block.
days.notes: Longest run remains 14 km. Next block will extend further (16–18 km) if building toward marathon peak.

coaching_overview: This 4-week Base Building / Aerobic Development block (June 22 – July 19, 2026) establishes a solid aerobic foundation for marathon training. The athlete starts from a very low recent training load (~10 km in 14 days), so week 1 is intentionally conservative (30.5 km, 5 runs) with strides-only quality to activate the neuromuscular system without fatigue. Weeks 2–3 build progressively: week 2 introduces tempo (6×2 min), week 3 extends to 3×4 min tempo and adds hill repeats (6×90 sec) for VO2 max development. Week 4 is a deload consolidation week (40 km, reduced intensity) allowing adaptation before the next macrocycle. Throughout, the training adheres to the 80/20 polarized model (~80% easy/aerobic, ~20% hard quality work) and respects the 7-hour weekly time budget (actual weekly totals: 2.5, 3.9, 4.5, 4.1 hours). Long runs progress from 8.5 km → 12 km → 14 km → 14 km (consolidation). By the end of week 4, the athlete will have built an aerobic base, introduced lactate threshold and VO2 max stimulus, and be ready for the next block—either extended Aerobic Development (if race is 8–12 weeks away) or Peak Phase (5–7 weeks away). Key coaching cues: focus on conversational easy paces (~6:15–6:20/km), maintain form and cadence, ensure adequate recovery and sleep, and hydrate consistently on long runs.
weekly_summary: Foundation week establishing aerobic base with easy runs and introductory strides. Low volume (30.5 km) over 5 days allows adaptation from recent light training load.
coaching_notes: Athlete has run only ~10 km in past 14 days, indicating a fresh start or recent layoff. This week is intentionally conservative to build momentum without overtraining. The 3.6 km and 6.3 km runs from late June show easy paces (5:50–6:10/km), so we establish a conversational baseline around 6:15/km for Zone 2. Two quality sessions (strides) introduce neuromuscular activation without fatigue.
recovery_flags: Recent training volume very low; watch for DOMS in week 2.

days.coach_analysis: You ran 6.31 km when a complete rest day was scheduled—this was an unplanned effort that broke the recovery protocol. The 6:10/km pace and 39-minute duration suggest an easy, conversational effort rather than hard running, which is good judgment on your part. However, rest days exist for a reason; next time, honor the plan with true recovery or communicate if you need to adjust your training schedule.

coach_adjustment.change: Stick to active recovery only (walk, easy 20-min jog max, or complete rest)
coach_adjustment.reason: Unplanned running on recovery days accumulates fatigue and undermines adaptation. Trust the plan.

days.title: Easy 5 km + 6×100m Strides
days.description: Repeat Wednesday structure: 5 km easy, then 6 × 100 m strides. Maintains neuromuscular stimulus while respecting recovery.
days.notes: Second quality session; 48 hours from Wednesday session.
days.coach_analysis: You completely substituted today's running workout with afternoon weight training instead. While strength work is valuable, this wasn't the planned quality session—you missed the neuromuscular stimulus from the strides and the easy aerobic base. If you needed to shift to weights today, that's fine, but we should understand why and ensure the running session gets rescheduled.

coach_adjustment.reason: Quality run was missed entirely today. This session is important for maintaining running fitness and neuromuscular sharpness—reschedule it before the weekend.
